g.data-narration: Welcome to Module 4 on Cloud Computing. Today we explore the Trusted Hypervisor, the security foundation of cloud infrastructure.
In multi-tenant clouds, multiple users share hardware. If the hypervisor is compromised, all tenant data becomes vulnerable.
A Trusted Hypervisor is a security-hardened VMM designed to resist attacks and form a Trusted Computing Base for cloud environments.
g.text: A security-hardened Virtual Machine Monitor (VMM) with enhanced security features designed to:
Forms the Trusted Computing Base (TCB) for cloud infrastructure

g.data-narration: The smaller the Trusted Computing Base, the fewer vulnerabilities. Trusted hypervisors use microkernel design to minimize code complexity.
g.text: TCB = All components critical to security. Smaller TCB = fewer vulnerabilities.

g.data-narration: Trusted hypervisors enforce isolation through CPU, memory, and I/O separation using hardware virtualization extensions like Intel VT-x and AMD-V.
Using TPM hardware, trusted hypervisors measure boot process hashes and store them in Platform Configuration Registers for remote attestation.
Hardware features like Intel TXT create trusted execution environments, while AMD SEV encrypts VM memory with unique keys for protection.
g.text: Encrypts VM memory - unreadable even to hypervisor

g.data-narration: Formal verification uses mathematical proofs to prove hypervisor code correctness, providing the highest security assurance level.
g.text: Mathematical proof that code correctly implements its specification - highest assurance level.

g.data-narration: Xen exemplifies trusted hypervisor design with its microkernel architecture - tiny core plus privileged Dom0 managing device drivers.
Compare hypervisors: Xen (microkernel, small TCB), VMware ESXi (monolithic, enterprise), KVM (monolithic, large TCB).
Key takeaways: Trusted hypervisors ensure secure multi-tenancy through minimal TCB, strict isolation, TPM attestation, and hardware security.
Trusted hypervisors protect financial, healthcare, and government workloads in the cloud, ensuring regulatory compliance and data privacy.
g.text: Trusted Hypervisor = Foundation of Secure Cloud Computing
